summary: Ronacher argues that understanding computing requires building intuition for scale, not just memorizing figures. He uses physical and everyday comparisons—like light travel distances per CPU tick—to make modern hardware speeds and sizes graspable. The post walks through CPUs, GPUs, game workloads, and algorithmic complexity to show how extreme these dimensions are. He emphasizes that comparisons help people reason, communicate, and avoid misleading numbers. The conclusion is that having mental models of magnitude speeds up problem‑solving and builds confidence, especially when exploring new domains like large‑scale voxel engines.
key_points: Physical comparisons (e.g., light travel per clock tick) make CPU scale comprehensible.
Modern games showcase massive parallel computation, bandwidth, and tight latency constraints.
Big‑O intuition improves when you relate complexity to limits like digit width or bounded input sizes.
Shared comparison libraries or “numbers everybody should know” help teams reason quickly.
Scale literacy prevents bad ideas, enables faster brainstorming, and improves confidence in discussions.
money_quotes: Numbers are quite meaningless unless you can compare them to something else you already know.
If a processor is ticking at 2.13 GHz it means that a single tick takes around 0.39 nanoseconds.
Yet our modern computers tick at a rate where for something we can measure light only moves a distance of about 10 centimeters.
If you keep in mind that numbers in computing are also very often limited in size you could think of the complexity of O(log n) as being nearly the same as O(1).
Having a basic idea of dimensions in computing makes it possible to brainstorm, accept and reject ideas without having to consult Wikipedia every few seconds.
Nothing feels more embarrassing than to suggest something completely out of proportions.
People love to confuse other people by throwing numbers around.
key_insight: Building intuition through concrete comparisons is the fastest way to reason about computing scale and make better engineering decisions.
